--- SOURCE 4: Low carbon concrete: advancements, challenges and ... ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y

SUMMARY:
Concrete production accounts for approximately 8% of global carbon emissions, underscoring the critical need for innovative low-carbon alternatives to support sustainable construction. The study examines emerging low-carbon concrete solutions, including the incorporation of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs), alkali-activated and geopolymer concrete, carbon capture and utilization (CCU), and recycled aggregates, which collectively show potential to reduce emissions by up to 30–50%. Although efforts are underway to develop alternative cement, enhance energy efficiency, and incorporate carbon capture technologies, significant challenges remain in reducing the overall environmental impact of concrete production. By incorporating waste products or less carbon-intensive ingredients, low-carbon concrete contributes to greener construction practices and helps mitigate climate change, aligning with global efforts to create more sustainable infrastructure.

FULL CONTENT:
Low carbon concrete: advancements, challenges and future directions in sustainable construction | Discover Concrete and Cement

Low carbon concrete: advancements, challenges and future directions in sustainable construction
===============================================================================================

*   Review

 Low carbon concrete: advancements, challenges and future directions in sustainable construction

Abstract
--------

Concrete production accounts for approximately 8% of global carbon emissions, underscoring the critical need for innovative low-carbon alternatives to support sustainable construction. This review addresses the environmental impact of conventional concrete and its substantial carbon footprint, emphasizing the urgency for transformative technologies. The study examines emerging low-carbon concrete solutions, including the incorporation of supplementary cementitious materials (SCMs), alkali-activated and geopolymer concrete, carbon capture and utilization (CCU), and recycled aggregates, which collectively show potential to reduce emissions by up to 30–50%. The paper also explores advanced mix design strategies aimed at minimizing emissions while maintaining or enhancing structural performance, which could improve durability by 20–25% and decrease lifecycle costs by up to 15%. Challenges to large-scale implementation are critically analyzed, including regulatory obstacles, technical constraints, and market dynamics, with a particular focus on achieving widespread market acceptance within a decade. Future research directions, such as material innovations, enhanced carbon capture methods, and the integration of digital technologies like AI for predictive modelling, are highlighted as pivotal for global adoption. This review demonstrates that the adoption of low-carbon concrete technologies is essential to achieving international climate targets, such as limiting global warming to 1.5°C while contributing to the construction sector’s sustainability. The findings provide a robust foundation for international research collaboration and policy development aimed at decarbonizing the construction industry.

1 Introduction
--------------

Climate change, driven by greenhouse gas emissions, is increasingly impacting the construction sector. The industry contributes significantly to emissions through energy use, material production, and waste generation. Buildings and infrastructure account for a large portion of global carbon footprints [[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR1 "Müller DB, Liu G, Løvik AN, Modaresi R, Pauliuk S, Steinhoff FS, Brattebø H. Carbon emissions of infrastructure development. Environ Sci Technol. 2013;47(20):11739–46."),[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR2 "Fenner AE, Kibert CJ, Woo J, Morque S, Razkenari M, Hakim H, Lu X. The carbon footprint of buildings: a review of methodologies and applications. Renew Sustain Energy Rev. 2018;94:1142–52."),[3](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR3 "Onat NC, Kucukvar M. Carbon footprint of construction industry: a global review and supply chain analysis. Renew Sustain Energy Rev. 2020;124: 109783."),[4](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR4 "Fang Z, Yan J, Lu Q, Chen L, Yang P, Tang J, Jiang F, Broyd T, Hong J. A systematic literature review of carbon footprint decision-making approaches for infrastructure and building projects. Appl Energy. 2023;335: 120768.")]. As climate change accelerates, construction practices face growing pressures to reduce environmental impact and adapt to changing conditions. This includes adopting energy-efficient technologies, sustainable materials, and resilient designs to withstand extreme weather events [[5](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR5 "Rathnam PM, Ram S. Imbibing energy efficiency in buildings through sustainable materials—a review. In: Advances in sustainable materials and resilient infrastructure. Singapore: Springer; 2022. p. 399–414."),[6](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR6 "Hafez FS, Sa’di B, Safa-Gamal M, Taufiq-Yap YH, Alrifaey M, Seyedmahmoudian M, Stojcevski A, Horan B, Mekhilef S. Energy efficiency in sustainable buildings: a systematic review with taxonomy, challenges, motivations, methodological aspects, recommendations, and pathways for future research. Energ Strat Rev. 2023;45: 101013."),[7](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR7 "Liu Z, Zhang X, Sun Y, Zhou Y. Advanced controls on energy reliability, flexibility, resilience, and occupant-centric control for smart and energy-efficient buildings—a state-of-the-art review. Energy Build. 2023;297: 113436."),[8](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR8 "Shashwat S, Zingre KT, Thurairajah N, Kumar DK, Panicker K, Anand P, Wan MP. A review on bioinspired strategies for an energy-efficient built environment. Energy Build. 2023;296: 113382."),[9](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR9 "Ahmadizadeh M, Heidari M, Thangavel S, Khashehchi M, Rahmanivahid P, Singh VP, Kumar A. Development of new materials for sustainable buildings. In: Swaroop C, editor. Sustainable technologies for energy efficient buildings. New York: CRC Press; 2024. p. 30–48.")]. The sector is critical in the fight against climate change, with the potential for substantial emissions reductions and enhanced sustainability through innovative practices and regulatory changes.

Concrete is a major contributor to global carbon emissions, accounting for about 8–10% of total emissions [[10](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR10 "Barcelo L, Kline J, Walenta G, Gartner E. Cement and carbon emissions. Mater Struct. 2014;47(6):1055–65."),[11](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR11 "Wu P, Xia B, Zhao X. The importance of use and end-of-life phases to the life cycle greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions of concrete–a review. Renew Sustain Energy Rev. 2014;37:360–9."),[12](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR12 "Akan MÖA, Dhavale DG, Sarkis J. Greenhouse gas emissions in the construction industry: an analysis and evaluation of a concrete supply chain. J Clean Prod. 2017;167:1195–207.")]. Its production process involves cement, a key ingredient that emits significant CO 2 when limestone is heated to produce clinker. The widespread use of concrete in construction, from buildings to infrastructure, amplifies its environmental impact. Efforts to mitigate this include developing low-carbon cement, incorporating recycled materials, and optimizing mix designs [[13](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR13 "Schneider M. The cement industry on the way to a low-carbon future. Cem Concr Res. 2019;124: 105792."),[14](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR14 "Bostanci SC, Limbachiya M, Kew H. Use of recycled aggregates for low carbon and cost effective concrete construction. J Clean Prod. 2018;189:176–96."),[15](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR15 "Orsini F, Marrone P. Approaches for a low-carbon production of building materials: a review. J Clean Prod. 2019;241: 118380."),[16](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR16 "Robalo K, Costa H, do Carmo, R. & Júlio, E. Experimental development of low cement content and recycled construction and demolition waste aggregates concrete. Constr Build Mater. 2021;273: 121680.")]. Despite advances, the sector faces challenges in reducing emissions due to concrete’s essential role in modern construction. Innovations and stricter regulations are crucial to addressing the environmental footprint of concrete.

The need for low-carbon concrete technologies is urgent due to the significant carbon footprint of traditional concrete production. Conventional concrete emits substantial CO 2 through cement manufacturing, a process that heats limestone to produce clinker. To combat climate change, it's crucial to develop and adopt low-carbon concrete alternatives. Innovations include using alternative materials like fly ash or slag, enhancing mix designs, and incorporating carbon capture technologies [[17](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR17 "Lee J, Lee T, Jeong J, Jeong J. Sustainability and performance assessment of binary blended low-carbon concrete using supplementary cementitious materials. J Clean Prod. 2021;280: 124373."),[18](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR18 "Thorne J, Bompa DV, Funari MF, Garcia-Troncoso N. Environmental impact evaluation of low-carbon concrete incorporating fly ash and limestone. Clean Mater. 2024;12: 100242."),[19](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR19 "Golafshani EM, Behnood A, Kim T, Ngo T, Kashani A. A framework for low-carbon mix design of recycled aggregate concrete with supplementary cementitious materials using machine learning and optimization algorithms. Structures. 2024;61: 106143."),[20](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR20 "Guo Y, Luo L, Liu T, Hao L, Li Y, Liu P, Zhu T. A review of low-carbon technologies and projects for the global cement industry. J Environ Sci. 2024;136:682–97.")]. These technologies can reduce emissions, improve sustainability, and support the construction industry's transition to greener practices. Embracing low-carbon concrete is essential for mitigating environmental impact and achieving global climate goals.

Low-carbon concrete has emerged as a vital innovation to reduce the carbon footprint of construction activities. Recent research highlights the potential of incorporating waste materials into concrete formulations to achieve environmental and mechanical performance goals. Tahwia et al. [[21](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR21 "Tahwia AM, Abdellatief M, Abd Elrahman M. Durability and ecological assessment of low-carbon high-strength concrete with short AR-glass fibers: effects of high-volume of solid waste materials. Constr Build Mater. 2024;429: 136422.")] demonstrate that high-strength concrete reinforced with alkali-resistant (AR) glass fibers and incorporating high volumes of solid waste materials exhibits significant durability under environmental stressors. This approach reduces reliance on energy-intensive cement production while improving long-term performance. Geopolymer composites, another avenue of sustainable concrete, show promise for their physical and mechanical robustness. Tahwia et al. [[22](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR22 "Tahwia AM, Aldulaimi DS, Abdellatief M, Youssf O. Physical, mechanical and durability properties of eco-friendly engineered geopolymer composites. Infrastructures. 2024;9(11):191.")] explored eco-friendly engineered geopolymer composites, emphasizing their enhanced durability and reduced carbon emissions. These materials offer a sustainable alternative to traditional cement by utilizing industrial by-products such as fly ash and slag. In another study, Abdellatief et al. [[23](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR23 "Abdellatief M, Adel B, Alanazi H, Tawfik TA. Multiscale optimization analysis of high strength alkali-activated concrete containing waste medical glass under exposure to carbonation and elevated temperatures. Dev Built Environ. 2024;19: 100492.")] investigated alkali-activated concrete incorporating waste medical glass, finding that such mixtures perform well under carbonation and elevated temperatures. This underscores their suitability for diverse environments while addressing waste management challenges. Additionally, advanced predictive modelling of foam concrete compressive strength, as analyzed by Abdellatief et al. [[24](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR24 "Abdellatief M, Wong LS, Din NM, Mo KH, Ahmed AN, El-Shafie A. Evaluating enhanced predictive modeling of foam concrete compressive strength using artificial intelligence algorithms. Mater Today Commun. 2024;40: 110022.")], highlights the role of artificial intelligence in optimizing material properties and design, paving the way for innovative low-carbon construction solutions.

The objective of this review is to evaluate and synthesize advancements in technologies and practices aimed at minimizing the carbon footprint of concrete production. It assesses low-carbon alternatives, such as innovative materials, mix designs, and production techniques, focusing on their effectiveness in reducing CO 2 emissions. The review covers recent innovations, comparative analyses, and their implications for the construction industry, emphasizing current research, challenges, and opportunities. By providing a holistic perspective, this study aims to enhance understanding of low-carbon concrete's role in promoting sustainable construction and supporting climate mitigation objectives.

2 Conventional concrete and its carbon footprint
------------------------------------------------

Traditional concrete production involves mixing cement, water, and aggregates (sand, gravel, or crushed stone) to create a durable building material. Cement, the primary component, is produced by heating limestone and other raw materials in a kiln to create clinker, which is then ground into a fine powder. This process is highly energy-intensive and contributes significantly to CO 2 emissions, as both limestone decomposition and fuel combustion release greenhouse gases [[25](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR25 "Poudyal L, Adhikari K. Environmental sustainability in cement industry: an integrated approach for green and economical cement production. Resour Environ Sustainabil. 2021;4: 100024."), [26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR26 "Nie S, Zhou J, Yang F, Lan M, Li J, Zhang Z, Chen Z, Xu M, Li H, Sanjayan JG. Analysis of theoretical carbon dioxide emissions from cement production: methodology and application. J Clean Prod. 2022;334: 130270.")]. The widespread use of concrete in construction is due to its strength and versatility, but its environmental impact highlights the urgent need for more sustainable production methods.

The raw materials for traditional concrete—cement, sand, gravel, and water—each have notable environmental impacts. Cement production is particularly carbon-intensive, with the heating of limestone to produce clinker emitting substantial amounts of CO 2. Sand and gravel extraction can lead to habitat destruction, water pollution, and soil erosion, while the energy required for mining and transporting these materials further contributes to emissions. Addressing these issues requires adopting more sustainable practices, such as using alternative materials or recycled content and improving resource management. Although efforts are underway to develop alternative cement, enhance energy efficiency, and incorporate carbon capture technologies, significant challenges remain in reducing the overall environmental impact of concrete production. Table [1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#Tab1) provides an overview of cement production and associated CO₂ emissions for different regions or countries, including the global average. The table gives a snapshot of production volume, total emissions, and emissions intensity per ton of cement, which can be useful for understanding regional variations in the environmental impact of cement production.

**Table 1 Global and regional overview of cement production and CO 2 emissions**

[Full size table](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y/tables/1)

Quantifying carbon emissions from conventional concrete involves assessing the entire lifecycle of concrete production, including raw material extraction, transportation, mixing, and curing. Cement production, a key component, is responsible for a significant portion of these emissions due to the high energy required and the chemical reactions involved. Typically, each ton of cement emits around 680–750 kg of CO₂ [[32](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR32 "Benhelal E, Zahedi G, Shamsaei E, Bahadori A. Global strategies and potentials to curb CO2 emissions in cement industry. J Clean Prod. 2013;51:142–61."),[33](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR33 "Andrew RM. Global CO2 emissions from cement production. Earth Syst Sci Data. 2018;10(1):195–217."),[34](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR34 "Shah IH, Miller SA, Jiang D, Myers RJ. Cement substitution with secondary materials can reduce annual global CO2 emissions by up to 1.3 gigatons. Nat Commun. 2022;13(1):5758.")]. Factors such as the efficiency of production processes, the use of alternative fuels, and the incorporation of supplementary materials can influence the overall carbon footprint. Accurate quantification is crucial for developing strategies to mitigate emissions and enhance sustainability in the construction industry.

In 2020, global CO 2 emissions were significantly contributed by China and India, accounting for an estimated 35%, while the UK, Germany, and France together contributed only 4% [[26](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR26 "Nie S, Zhou J, Yang F, Lan M, Li J, Zhang Z, Chen Z, Xu M, Li H, Sanjayan JG. Analysis of theoretical carbon dioxide emissions from cement production: methodology and application. J Clean Prod. 2022;334: 130270.")]. Between 2005 and 2021, the highest CO 2 emitters were China, India, Europe, and the USA (Fig.[1](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#Fig1)) [[35](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR35 "International Energy Agency (IEA). 2009 Cement roadmap targets. Paris. Available at:
                  https://iea.blob.core.windows.net/assets/cbaa3da1-fd61-4c2a-8719-3153859b54f/TechnologyRoadmapLowCarbonTransitionintheCementIndustry.pdf
                  
                . Accessed: 5 Sept 2024.")]. During the COVID-19 pandemic, emissions from the cement sector remained relatively stable, showing less decline compared to sectors like oil, gas, and coal [[36](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR36 "Science & Nature. 2022 'Cement Carbon Dioxide Emissions Quietly Double in 20 Years'. WTTW News. Available at: 
                  https://news.wttw.com/2022/06/22/cement-carbon-dioxide-emissions-quietly-double-20-years
                  
                . Accessed: 5 Sept 2024.")]. However, by implementing strategies such as using alternative fuels, improving energy efficiency, substituting clinker, and employing carbon capture and utilization (CCU), cement industry CO 2 emissions could be reduced to 1.5 billion tons per year and 0.43 tons of CO 2 per ton of cement by 2050 (Fig.[2](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#Fig2)) [[37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR37 "Our World in Data. Annual CO2 emissions from cement. 2022; Available at: 
                  https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/annual-co2-cement?tab=chart
                  
                . Accessed 5 Sept 2024.")]. Heidelberg Cement has committed to reducing CO 2 emissions by 25–30% by 2030 compared to 1999 levels and was the first company to receive approval for science-based CO 2 capture targets. Various CO 2 capture technologies, including amine scrubbing, calcium looping, oxyfuel combustion, and direct separation, have been evaluated. However, pre-combustion processes are not ideal for the cement industry, as they are better suited for capturing energy-related CO 2 emissions [[38](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR38 "Miyamoto O, Maas C, Tsujiuchi T, Inui M, Hirata T, Tanaka H, Yonekawa T, Kamijo T. KM CDR ProcessTM project update and the new novel solvent development. Energy Procedia. 2017;114:5616–23.")].

Globally CO 2 emission from 1990 to 2050 with and without any mitigation strategies [[37](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR37 "Our World in Data. Annual CO2 emissions from cement. 2022; Available at: 
                  https://ourworldindata.org/grapher/annual-co2-cement?tab=chart
                  
                . Accessed 5 Sept 2024.")]

3 Low carbon concrete: definitions and concepts
-----------------------------------------------

Low-carbon concrete is a sustainable alternative to traditional concrete that significantly reduces carbon dioxide emissions during its production. Unlike conventional concrete, which relies heavily on Portland cement—a major source of CO 2 emissions—low-carbon concrete uses materials like fly ash, slag, or alternative cement to lower its carbon footprint. These materials not only reduce greenhouse gas emissions but also often enhance the concrete's performance and durability. By incorporating waste products or less carbon-intensive ingredients, low-carbon concrete contributes to greener construction practices and helps mitigate climate change, aligning with global efforts to create more sustainable infrastructure.

Carbon footprint reduction strategies aim to minimize greenhouse gas emissions across various sectors. Key strategies include adopting renewable energy sources like solar or wind, improving energy efficiency in buildings and industrial processes, and transitioning to low-carbon transportation options such as electric vehicles [[39](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR39 "Oró E, Depoorter V, Garcia A, Salom J. Energy efficiency and renewable energy integration in data centres. Strategies and modelling review. Renew Sustain Energy Rev. 2015;42:429–45."),[40](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR40 "Ruparathna R, Hewage K, Sadiq R. Improving the energy efficiency of the existing building stock: a critical review of commercial and institutional buildings. Renew Sustain Energy Rev. 2016;53:1032–45."),[41](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR41 "Colmenar-Santos A, Muñoz-Gómez AM, Rosales-Asensio E, López-Rey Á. Electric vehicle charging strategy to support renewable energy sources in Europe 2050 low-carbon scenario. Energy. 2019;183:61–74."),[42](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s44416-025-00002-y#ref-CR42 "Bonsu NO. Towards a circular and low-carbon economy: insights from the transitioning to electric vehicles and net zero economy. J Clean Prod. 2020;256: 120659.")]. Additionally, practices such as reducing waste, recycling materials, and using energy-efficient appliances contribute to lowering emissions. Supporting sustainable agriculture and conserving natural habitats also play crucial roles. By integrating these strategies, individuals and organizations can significantly reduce their carbon footp

# Embodied carbon saving potential of using recycled materials as cement substitute in Singapore’s buildings

[*npj Materials Sustainability*](/npjmatsustain)
**volume 2**, Article number: 27 (2024)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

5795 Accesses

8 Citations

[Metrics details](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

Material production and construction activities are key contributors to global carbon footprints, necessitating sustainable alternatives. This study aims to investigate the potential of integrating recycled materials as Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs) in concrete production to mitigate the substantial carbon emissions of Singapore’s building and construction sector. The research focuses on Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag (GGBFS), waste glass powder, and calcined marine clay as alternative SCMs, aiming to reduce environmental impact and waste disposal emissions in Singapore. Employing a cradle-to-gate Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology for 1 m3 of concrete with different grades, this study quantifies embodied carbon savings and assesses the feasibility of substituting these SCMs in concrete. The results reveal that substituting Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) with GGBFS in concrete offers the most significant reduction, up to 56%, in 1 m3 of concrete. In contrast, the use of calcined marine clay and glass powder in concrete results in reductions of up to 21% and 16%, respectively. Two case studies were used to exemplify the impact of using SCM concrete at the project scale. Results indicate that up to 31% of the total embodied carbon could be saved in the building. Additionally, scenario analysis suggests that the total emissions from cementitious materials in Singapore could decrease by 20% through the incorporation of locally recycled marine clay and glass powder. This reduction could potentially reach 56% if the GGBFS supply is not constrained. To further enhance sustainability in Singapore’s construction sector, the study proposes sourcing GGBFS from neighboring countries to minimize transportation emissions and localizing the production and usage of calcined marine clay and glass powder. These measures can improve material circularity and significantly contribute to achieving carbon reduction targets.

## Introduction

The building and construction industry is a significant contributor to global carbon emissions, responsible for 39% of these emissions. Within this sector, carbon emissions from the production of building materials and construction activities account for 11% of the total[1](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR1 "World Green Building Council. Bringing Embodied Carbon Upfront. 
                  www.worldgbc.org/embodied-carbon
                  
                 (2019)."). This subset of emissions, known as embodied carbon, has garnered increasing attention due to its rising share of the total carbon emissions from buildings. According to the World Green Building Council (WGBC)[1](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR1 "World Green Building Council. Bringing Embodied Carbon Upfront. 
                  www.worldgbc.org/embodied-carbon
                  
                 (2019)."), it is projected that by 2050, the embodied impact of new constructions could contribute up to 50% of the total carbon emissions.

The use of low-carbon building materials is identified as a key strategy in reducing embodied carbon in building industry[2](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR2 "Giesekam, J., Barrett, J. R. & Taylor, P. Construction sector views on low carbon building materials. Build. Res. Inf. 44, 423–444 (2016)."), with production falling into two primary approaches. The first approach focuses on using alternative energy sources or innovative technologies to reduce carbon emissions during the material production stage. In cement industry, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions due to fossil fuel combustion in kiln could contribute to 40% of the GHG emissions of cement production[3](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR3 "Imbabi, M. S., Carrigan, C. & McKenna, S. Trends and developments in green cement and concrete technology. Int. J. Sustain. Built Environ. 1, 194-216 (2012)."). Thus, efforts have been made to adopt alternative fuel in cement production. Clark et al.[4](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR4 "Clark, G., Davis, M., Shibani & Kumar, A. Assessment of fuel switching as a decarbonization strategy in the cement sector. Energy Convers. Manag 312, 118585 (2024).") stated that the cumulative energy-related GHG emissions in cement industry could be up to 21% between 2020 and 2050 by fuel switching. Georgiopoulou et al.[5](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR5 "Georgiopoulou, M. & Lyberatos, G. Life cycle assessment of the use of alternative fuels in cement kilns: A case study. J. Environ. Manag. 216, 224–234 (2018).") reported a 22% reduction of carbon emission in cement kiln by substituting 30% of fossil fuel by biofuel. Zhang et al.[6](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR6 "Zhang, L. & Mabee, W. E. Comparative study on the life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions of the utilization of potential low carbon fuels for the cement industry. J. Clean. Prod. 122, 102–112 (2016).") reported a 40% embodied carbon reduction in cement production when 100% fossil fuels are replaced by construction and demolition (C&D) wood waste. Carbon Capture Utilization and Storage (CCUS) also play a crucial role in decarbonizing industries like cement and steel production. The aim of CCUS is to capture the carbon emission during material production to prevent it from leaking to the environment. Then the carbon will be utilized for chemical processing or stored in other places. In roadmap for cement low-carbon transition proposed by International Energy Agency (IEA), CCUS contributes 48% of the overall carbon reduction by 2050[7](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR7 "IEA; WBCSD. Technology Roadmap for Cement. International Energy Agency (2018)."). Baker et al.[8](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR8 "Baker, R. W., Freeman, B., Kniep, J., Huang, Y. I. & Merkel, T. C. CO2 Capture from Cement Plants and Steel Mills Using Membranes. Ind. Eng. Chem. Res 57, 15963–15970 (2018).") examines the carbon-reduction potential of membrane-based CCUS system, which could capture 80% of carbon emitted from cement or steel industry.

Despite the great potential of utilizing alternative fuels and CCUS technology to mitigate carbon emission, implementing these decarbonization methods may be limited to countries with large-scale production capabilities. In countries like Singapore, where a considerable amount of building materials is imported, alternative strategies must be adopted. Therefore, this paper primarily concentrates on the second approach, which involves incorporating recycled substitutes into building material production.

The use of recycled substitutes in building materials offers substantial potential for reducing embodied carbon. By 2050, it is anticipated that 37% of carbon emission in cement industry could be saved by substituting clinker with SCMs[7](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR7 "IEA; WBCSD. Technology Roadmap for Cement. International Energy Agency (2018)."). Commonly used SCMs in concrete production include silica fume (SF)[9](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR9 "Mohan, A. & Mini, K. M. Strength and durability studies of SCC incorporating silica fume and ultra fine GGBS. Constr. Build Mater. 171, 919–928 (2018)."), fly ash (FA)[10](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR10 "Diaz-Loya, I., Juenger, M., Seraj, S. & Minkara, R. Extending supplementary cementitious material resources: Reclaimed and remediated fly ash and natural pozzolans. Cem. Concr. Compos 101, 44–51 (2019).") and GGBFS[11](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR11 "Samad, S. & Shah, A. Role of binary cement including Supplementary Cementitious Material (SCM), in production of environmentally sustainable concrete: A critical review. Int. J. Sustain. Built Environ. 6 (2017)."). Among which GGBFS is the most widely used SCM in Singapore. GGBFS is a by-product of steel-making process. Slagging agents are added to the blast furnace or basic oxygen furnace to remove the impurities of iron ore during smelting. The slag lid floats on the liquid metal to protect it from outside oxygen and remain temperature. The slag could be easily removed from the surface of the metal. The removed slag will undergo quick water quenching to form a granular product that will be further grounded to fine powders[11](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR11 "Samad, S. & Shah, A. Role of binary cement including Supplementary Cementitious Material (SCM), in production of environmentally sustainable concrete: A critical review. Int. J. Sustain. Built Environ. 6 (2017)."),[12](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR12 "Ahmad, J. et al. A Comprehensive Review on the Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS) in Concrete Production. Sustainability (Switz.) 14, 8783 (2022)."). Concrete incorporating GGBFS have generally slower hydration process, resulting in a reduced strength in early stage. But ultimate strength of concrete will be increased due to the pozzolanic behavior of slag in slag-cement blend[11](#ref-CR11 "Samad, S. & Shah, A. Role of binary cement including Supplementary Cementitious Material (SCM), in production of environmentally sustainable concrete: A critical review. Int. J. Sustain. Built Environ. 6 (2017)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Ahmad, J. et al. A Comprehensive Review on the Ground Granulated Blast Furnace Slag (GGBS) in Concrete Production. Sustainability (Switz.) 14, 8783 (2022)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Zhou, X. M., Slater, J. R., Wavell, S. E. & Oladiran, O. Effects of PFA and GGBS on early-ages engineering properties of Portland cement systems. J. Adv. Concr. Technol. 10, 74–85 (2012)."),[14](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR14 "Siddique, R. & Bennacer, R. Use of iron and steel industry by-product (GGBS) in cement paste and mortar. Resources, Conserv. Recycl. 69 (2012)."). As GGBFS replace the cement in the concrete it tends to improve the concrete pore structure and Interfacial Transition Zone (ITZ) turns out to be denser[15](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR15 "Duan, P., Shui, Z., Chen, W. & Shen, C. Enhancing microstructure and durability of concrete from ground granulated blast furnace slag and metakaolin as cement replacement materials. J. Mater. Res. Technol. 2, 52–59 (2013)."). As a result, inclusion of GGBFS could reduce the permeability and provide better chemical resistance thus improving the durability of concrete[16](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR16 "Hussain, F., Kaur, I. & Hussain, A. Reviewing the influence of GGBFS on concrete properties. Mater. Today.: Proc. 32, 997–1004 (2020).").

Besides of GGBFS, Singapore has also conducted research of utilizing local waste like glass powder and marine clay into concrete production to promotes local material circularity. Du et al[17](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR17 "Du, H. & Tan, K. H. Waste glass powder as cement replacement in concrete. J. Adv. Concr. Technol. 12, 468–477 (2014)."). collected waste soda lime glasses from a local recycler in Singapore. Crusher and ball miller were used to break and finely grind the recycled glass into powders until the desired fineness was achieved. Du and Tan[18](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR18 "Du, H. & Tan, K. H. Transport properties of concrete with glass powder as supplementary cementitious material. ACI Mater. J. 112, 9558–9562 (2015)."),[19](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR19 "Du, H. & Tan, K. H. Properties of high volume glass powder concrete. Cem. Concr. Compos 75, 22–29 (2017).") investigated the mechanical and transport and durability properties of concrete with up to 60% finely ground glass powder replacing OPC. If the replacement is below 30%, due to the pozzolanic reaction between glass powder and cement hydration products, the 28-days compressive strength of the concrete will not be decreased by the substitution of glass powder. Additionally, the resistance to chloride ions and water penetration continuously increases with higher glass powder content. These improvements in durability properties are due to the refined microstructures, particularly at the interfacial transition zone. Hassani et al.[20](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR20 "Hassani, M. S., Matos, J. C., Zhang, Y. & Teixeira, E. R. Green Concrete with Glass Powder—A Literature Review. Sustainability 15, 14864 (2023).") conducted a literature review on the use of glass powder in concrete through 78 published scientific articles. They concluded that incorporating waste glass powder has advantages in higher mechanical and durability properties as well as lower environmental impact.

Marine clay, a type of low-grade kaolinite clay commonly found in coastal areas worldwide, is typically produced during excavation activities. These clays are characterized by their high silt content and low kaolinite content, making them of limited value for industrial applications[21](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR21 "Du, H., Dixit, A. & Pang, S. D. Potential of Marine Clay for Cement Replacement and Pozzolanic Additive in Concrete. RILEM Bookseries 25, 57–65 (2020)."). However, recent scientific research has explored their potential utility in several areas, including as a partial replacement for cement, as an additive to enhance the pozzolanic properties of cement[22](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR22 "Du, H. & Pang, S. D. Value-added utilization of marine clay as cement replacement for sustainable concrete production. J. Clean. Prod. 198, 867–873 (2018).") or silica sand[23](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR23 "Chen, W., Wang, Q., Huang, Z. & Du, H. Efficient utilization of waste marine clay for fine aggregate to develop sustainable and cost-effective strain-hardening cement-based composites. Constr. Build Mater. 417, 135262 (2024)."), as a component in low-carbon lightweight strain-hardening cement composites[24](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR24 "Chen, W., Dang, J. & Du, H. Using low-grade calcined clay to develop low-carbon and lightweight strain-hardening cement composites. J. Build. Eng. 58, 105023 (2022)."), and as a filler replacement in ultra-high-performance concrete[22](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR22 "Du, H. & Pang, S. D. Value-added utilization of marine clay as cement replacement for sustainable concrete production. J. Clean. Prod. 198, 867–873 (2018)."). In studies by Du et al.[21](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR21 "Du, H., Dixit, A. & Pang, S. D. Potential of Marine Clay for Cement Replacement and Pozzolanic Additive in Concrete. RILEM Bookseries 25, 57–65 (2020)."),[22](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR22 "Du, H. & Pang, S. D. Value-added utilization of marine clay as cement replacement for sustainable concrete production. J. Clean. Prod. 198, 867–873 (2018)."), marine clay, initially supplied in large lumps, underwent a process where it was dried at 50 °C for three days, manually pulverized, and then ground in a ball mill for 30 min. The resulting fine powder was subsequently calcined at specific temperatures (600 °C, 700 °C, and 800 °C) for one hour in a furnace. During this process, the heating ramp was maintained at 10 °C/min from room temperature to the designated calcination temperature. Afterward, the calcined clay was removed from the furnace and rapidly cooled by spreading it on a metal tray at room temperature. According to Du and Pang[22](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR22 "Du, H. & Pang, S. D. Value-added utilization of marine clay as cement replacement for sustainable concrete production. J. Clean. Prod. 198, 867–873 (2018)."), without pre-treatment, kaolinite in marine clay is approximate 20% by mass. Owing to the low kaolinite content, the pozzolanic reaction could be completed within the first week. The calcined clay mortar with 30% replacement ratio shows a high strength activation index of approximately 0.9. Raw marine clay samples can also be transformed into a value-added product through 2 h thermal treatment at 700 °C, enhancing their pozzolanic activity and accelerating heat flow and calorimetric responses[25](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR25 "Dang, J., Du, H. & Pang, S. D. Hydration, strength and microstructure evaluation of eco-friendly mortar containing waste marine clay. J. Clean. Prod. 272, 122784 (2020)."). Calcined marine clay refines mortar pores, reducing threshold, critical and average pore sizes, but increasing total pore volume and porosity with higher replacement ratios. The compressive and flexural strengths decrease as the cement replacement ratio increases. Compared to the reference, mortar with 15% fine calcined marine clay achieves the highest strength. However, the strength significantly drops beyond 45% replacement due to dilution, porous structure, and agglomeration effects.

Previous studies have examined the embodied carbon saving potential of using SCMs in concrete production. Crossin[26](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR26 "Crossin, E. The greenhouse gas implications of using ground granulated blast furnace slag as a cement substitute. J. Clean. Prod. 95, 101–108 (2015).") examined the carbon emission of 1 m3 of concrete adopting 70% of GGBFS substitution based on Australian database, which resulted in 48% of carbon reduction comparing to OPC concrete. Tait and Cheung[27](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR27 "Tait, M. W. & Cheung, W. M. A comparative cradle-to-gate life cycle assessment of three concrete mix designs. Int. J. Life Cycle Assess. 21, 847–860 (2016).") studied a cradle to gate concrete containing 30% OPC and 70% GGBFS and reported a 62% of carbon reduction based on UK context. Regarding lower GGBFS replacement ratio, Aslani et al.[28](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR28 "Aslani, A., Hachem-Vermette, C. & Zahedi, R. Environmental impact assessment and potentials of material efficiency using by-products and waste materials. Constr. Build Mater. 378, 131197 (2023).") assessed the environmental impact of concrete mix using by-products and waste materials, including GGBFS, FA and glass powder. They implemented cradle-to-gate LCA adopting Ecoinvent 3 database and concluded that GGBFS concrete with 50% replacement ratio could achieve the highest carbon reduction compared with other waste/by product concrete, which could reach 41% when compared to controlled OPC concrete. Hossain et al.[29](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR29 "Hossain, M. U., Cai, R., Ng, S. T., Xuan, D. & Ye, H. Sustainable natural pozzolana concrete – A comparative study on its environmental performance against concretes with other industrial by-products. Constr. Build Mater. 270, 121429 (2021).") reported 28% of GHG reduction in 50% GGBFS concrete using data from Chinese Life Cycle Database (CLCD).

It is noted that the carbon saving potential of SCM concrete varies significantly according to the studied region. This is mainly resulted from the regional technology variance and material transportation distance[30](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR30 "Gursel, A. P. & Ostertag, C. P. Impact of Singapore’s importers on life-cycle assessment of concrete. J. Clean. Prod. 118, 140–150 (2016)."). To address this issue and achieve more accurate environmental impact evaluation, it is necessary to quantify the localized embodied carbon saving potential of the SCMs based on local context. This research employed cradle-to-gate life cycle assessment (LCA) to assess the embodied carbon of various SCM concrete mixtures based on their functional performance in Singapore. Two local case studies are utilized to exemplify their embodied carbon saving potential. Scenario analysis reflecting various SCM adopting level and sustainable strategies are performed. This assessment will enable a more informed comparison and facilitate strategic decision-making regarding SCM adoption in Singapore’s construction sector.

## Results

### ECF of OPC and SCMs

The embodied carbon emission of waste glass powder, calcined marine clay, GGBFS and OPC are assessed based on Singapore’s local context and quantified by embodied carbon factor (ECF). The assessment includes the material production and transportation process. Life cycle inventory (LCI) data of these material are listed in Tables [1](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab1), [2](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab2), [3](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab3) to Table [4](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab4). The ECF assessment of waste glass powder and marine clay are not considering the raw material extraction process, since they are treated as local wastes; yet, the additional processing (e.g., grinding to the appropriate fineness of waste glass powder) are considered in the analysis. The LCI data of waste glass powder are retrived from the paper in preperation by H. Du. The ECF of OPC and GGBFS over past 20 years are assessed based on their import amount and the corresponding local production emissions in original country, as shown in Figs. [1](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig1), [2](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig2). No allocation and economic allocation are separately applied to GGBFS assessment. We assume the importing structure of OPC and GGBFS from the past 3 years are representative for future analysis. Thus, the average ECF of OPC and GGBFS in past three years are adopted in the following discussion and case studies. The detailed import data and the corresponding ECF of OPC and GGBFS are listed in Tables [3](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab3), [4](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab4), respectively.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-024-00032-w/MediaObjects/44296_2024_32_Fig1_HTML.png)

The changing importing structure of OPC has no strong impact on its ECF.

![figure 2](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-024-00032-w/MediaObjects/44296_2024_32_Fig2_HTML.png)

The significant change of GGBFS ECF indicates the strong impact of importing structure on the embodied carbon emission of GGBFS in Singapore. The economic allocation method generates higher ECF results for GGBFS than no allocation method.

### Embodied carbon emission of concrete mixtures

This research applied cradle-to-gate LCA methodology to assess the embodied carbon emission of 1 m3 of concrete with different grades (i.e., G40, G50 and G60). The impact of cement substitution using GGBFS, calcined marine clay and glass powder was also evaluated for each concrete grade. The assessment results are shown in Fig. [3](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig3). It can be observed that embodied carbon of concrete generally rises with higher grade, due to the increasing cement content. Among all SCMs, GGBFS could achieve the highest embodied carbon reduction regardless of the allocation method. The embodied carbon reduction for no allocation GGBFS concrete is 55% to 56% and for economic allocation GGBFS concrete is 53% to 54%. Marine clay concrete could achieve 20% to 21% emission reduction while glass powder concrete could generally achieve 16% of reduction. The reduction ratio of using SCMs slightly varies with concrete grades. Generally, higher grade concrete with more cement content could exhibit more significant emission reduction, since more cement content could be replaced by SCMs.

![figure 3](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-024-00032-w/MediaObjects/44296_2024_32_Fig3_HTML.png)

The embodied carbon emission of concrete is primarily impacted by the concrete strength and SCM usage.

### Case studies

In order to exemplify the embodied carbon saving potential of using recycled materials in SCM concrete, case studies were conducted using two local projects adopting different structural systems.

Case study 1 involved a 19-storey composite building with 4-level basements. The GFA of this project is 186,166 m2. This project utilized composite columns and composite slabs in superstructure and cast in-situ reinforced concrete (RC) slabs in the basement. The concrete strength for all superstructure elements and basement elements (except for basement walls) is Grade 40. The basement wall from basement 4 to basement 1 utilized G60 concrete. All steel elements in composite structural members adopt S355 steel. Due to the limited data available, only main structural elements are considered in embodied carbon calculation including columns, beams, slabs and walls. The material information for the structural elements is estimated and tabulated in Table [5](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab5). The structural foundation as well as the interior decoration materials are ignored in this case study. The rebar volume is estimated as 2% of total concrete volume. The steel amounts are provided by the designer. The ECF of rebar and steel is taken as 2.51 kgCO2e/kg and 2.84 kgCO2e/kg from SGBC calculator[31](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR31 "SGBC. Singapore Building Carbon Calculator. 
                  https://carboncalculator.sg/
                  
                ."). The density of rebar and steel is assumed to be 7850 kg/m3.

Case study 2 (Fig. [4](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig4)) involved a 9-story RC building with GFA of 30,972 m2. This project adopted conventional RC frame structure, with Grade 60 concrete utilized in structural columns and walls, Grade 50 concrete utilized in structural beams and slabs, and Grade 40 concrete utilized in stairs. Similar to case study 1, the foundations and interior decoration materials are ignored. The material information for case study 2 is listed in Table [6](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab6).

![figure 4](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs44296-024-00032-w/MediaObjects/44296_2024_32_Fig4_HTML.png)

Typical structual members in case study 2, including columns, beams, slabs and walls.

Concrete adopting three kinds of recycled SCMs are applied to the case study according to its strength. The usage of rebar and steel section are assumed to be unchanged. The embodied carbon intensity (ECI) of original projects, which is quoted as kgCO2e/m2, is compared with that of the building after substitution of SCM concrete. The results for two case studies are listed in Tables [7](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab7), [8](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Tab8). These ECI results are further compared with Singapore benchmark as shown in Fig. [5](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig5). Singapore BCA[32](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#ref-CR32 "Building and Construction Authority. Greenmark 2021 Technical Guide.") set 1000 kgCO2e/m2 as the benchmark of non-residential buildings. It is observed that the original building is already below the BCA benchmark. However, the ECI value could be further reduced by adopting SCM concrete in the building. In case study 1, it is observed that the GGBFS concrete achieved the highest reduction of 25% and 24% for no allocation method and economic allocation method, respectively. Marine clay concrete achieved 9% of overall ECI reduction and Glass powder concrete achieved 7% reduction. For case study 2, GGBFS concrete achieved 31% and 30% of reduction. Marine clay concrete and glass powder concrete achieved 12% and 9% of reduction, respectively. For both case studies, adopting GGBFS concrete could result in the highest embodied carbon saving, followed by marine clay concrete and glass powder concrete. Besides, the carbon saving potential of using SCM concrete in case study 2 is generally higher than in case study 1, which may be due to the difference in structural system. Case study 1 adopted composite structure, which includes large amount of structural steel usage. As shown in Fig. [6](/articles/s44296-024-00032-w#Fig6), OPC concrete, rebar and steel contributes to 46%, 43% and 11% of overall embodied carbon emission. For RC structure in case study 2, the OPC concrete and rebar contribute to 56% and 44%. The higher concrete usage i

enriched_points.point_title: Sustainable concrete composites reduce carbon emissions
enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative concrete formulations, such as those incorporating biochar or carbonated materials, can significantly lower carbon footprints, achieving reductions of up to 56% compared to traditional methods.
The integration of supplementary cementitious materials and advanced mix designs not only reduces emissions but also improves concrete's durability and strength, thus enhancing overall performance.
Case studies show substantial embodied carbon reductions, with GGBFS concrete achieving up to 31% less emissions, proving the viability of sustainable composites in construction.

enriched_points.point_title: Future research is essential for broader adoption
enriched_points.expanded_content: Continued exploration of material innovations, carbon capture technologies, and regulatory frameworks is critical for overcoming challenges and facilitating the widespread use of sustainable concrete solutions.
